en.extract:
The Australian white ibis (Threskiornis molucca) is a wading bird of the family Threskiornithidae. It is widespread across much of Australia. It has a predominantly white plumage with a bare, black head, long downcurved bill, and black legs. While it is closely related to the African sacred ibis, the Australian white ibis is a native Australian bird. Contrary to urban myth, it is not a feral species introduced to Australia by people, and it does not come from Egypt.
Historically rare in urban areas, the Australian white ibis has established in urban areas of the east coast in increasing numbers since the late 1970s; it is now commonly seen in Wollongong, Sydney, Melbourne, Adelaide, Darwin, the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Townsville. In recent years, the bird has also become increasingly common in Perth, Western Australia, and surrounding towns in south-western Australia. Populations have disappeared from natural breeding areas such as the Macquarie Marshes in northern New South Wales. Management plans have been introduced to control problematic urban populations in Sydney.
Due to its increasing presence in the urban environment and its habit of rummaging in garbage, the species has acquired a variety of colloquial names such as "tip turkey" and "bin chicken", and in recent years has become an icon of Australia's popular culture, regarded with glee by some and passionate revulsion by others.


Taxonomy
It was initially described by Georges Cuvier in 1829 as Ibis molucca. It is considered part of a superspecies complex with the sacred ibis (T. aethiopicus) of Africa, and the black-headed ibis (T. melanocephalus) of Asia. Its status in the complex has vacillated over the years. Many older guidebooks referred to the bird as a species, T. molucca, until a comprehensive review of plumage patterns by Holyoak in 1970. Holyoak noted the three species' similarities and that the Australian taxon resembled T. aethiopicus in adult plumage and T. melanocephalus in juvenile plumage. He proposed that they all be considered part of a single species, T. aethiopicus. This was generally accepted by the scientific community until Lowe and Richards's assessment of plumage in 1991. They again recommended the recognition of molucca at species level. This was followed by a chromosome study, which highlighted each of the three species having a different karyotype. The Australian white ibis has been considered a full species by most authorities since then.


Subspecies
Two subspecies are recognised:

T. m. molucca of eastern Malesia and Australia, is the nominate subspecies.
T. m. pygmaeus (Solomons white ibis), known locally as the tagoa, is a dwarf form found on the Solomon Islands that has been considered a separate species (as Threskiornis pygmaeus) at times. It is found only on Rennell and Bellona Islands, in the Solomon Islands of Melanesia, in the south-west Pacific Ocean.


Description
The Australian white ibis is a fairly large ibis species, around 65–75 cm (26–30 in) long and has a bald black head and neck and a long black downcurved beak, measuring over 16.7 cm (6.6 in) in the male, and under in the female. There is some sexual dimorphism in size, as the slightly heavier male weighs 1.7–2.5 kg (3.7–5.5 lb) compared to the 1.4–1.9 kg (3.1–4.2 lb) female. As a comparison, the American white ibis generally attains 1 kg (2.2 lb) in weight. The body plumage is white, although it may become brown-stained. Inner secondary plumes are displayed as lacy black "tail" feathers. The upper tail becomes yellow when the bird is breeding. The legs and feet are dark and red skin is visible on the underside of the wing. Immature birds have shorter bills. The head and neck are feathered in juveniles.

The call is a long croak.
The Australian white ibis reaches sexual maturity in three years, and can reach twenty-eight years of age.


Distribution

The Australian white ibis is widespread in eastern, northern and south-western Australia. It occurs in marshy wetlands, often near open grasslands and has become common in Australian east-coast city parks and rubbish dumps in the urban areas of Wollongong, Sydney, Perth, the Gold Coast, Brisbane and Townsville. Historically, it was rare in urban areas – the first visits were noted after drought drove birds eastwards in the late 1970s, and there were no breeding records in Sydney until the 1980s.
The Macquarie Marshes in north-western New South Wales used to be one of the main breeding areas of the bird, with 11,000 nests reported in 1998. However, since 2000, none have been reported breeding there. The species is absent from Tasmania.
Populations are known to have boomed, particularly in urban areas, in the 1970s, early 2000s, and around 2010. However, there is no national monitoring of the species, and therefore no official population counts.


Urban populations

The species has been able to colonise urban areas by reducing its fear response when in close proximity to humans, and by significantly widening its suite of food items to include human refuse – strategies that other closely related species such as the straw-necked ibis and the spoonbills have not replicated.
Questions surrounding the origins of recent highly urbanised and closely human-habituated populations of the species are complicated by the establishment of free-flying exhibit flocks of formerly captive birds at a number of zoos and wildlife parks, including Sydney's Taronga Zoo, which first acquired birds for this purpose around 1971. A 1973 ABC TV report noted Taronga's by then well-established "liberty flock" was breeding locally, unlike natural populations, which at that time were only known to fleetingly visit the urban area and not breed there.
The resident Taronga flock nested in exotic Canary Island date palms, and was notably very closely habituated to people; approaching them at close quarters, feeding from rubbish bins and scavenging food from outdoor dining areas. These previously undocumented behaviours became closely associated with the urban Sydney flocks that emerged from around 1980 onwards, directly across the harbour in the Royal Botanic Gardens and the Sydney CBD, and further afield in the Centennial Parklands.
The emergence of urban ibis populations in the wake of the 1970s zoo liberty flock experiment directly mirrors the establishment of the African sacred ibis as a resident species across Europe, with those populations confirmed as originating from free ranging zoo flocks in the same decade.
The relationship between birds originating from the Taronga flock and any influx of inland birds into Sydney is poorly known, but it is speculated that human-habituated flocks originating from the zoo may have encouraged some visiting flocks to stay in Sydney, with the two populations likely merging to some degree. Resident Sydney birds may have influenced newcomers fleeing from inland drought to adapt to new food sources in the city and to accept close proximity to humans.
Other free-flying exhibit populations were similarly established at Healesville Sanctuary in Victoria and at Tidbinbilla in the ACT, both of which mirrored the Taronga example by appearing to serve as population nucleation points. Healesville birds also seeded a free-flying population at Currumbin Wildlife Sanctuary in Queensland.
The urban population further increased after a further period of inland drought in 1998. The birds are an example of wildlife migration caused by climate change. The first big colony set up in the Sydney suburb of Bankstown and started to cause anxiety in the local community. It is estimated the colony was the largest outside the Macquarie Marshes, their natural breeding wetland in inland NSW.


Behaviour


Feeding
The Australian white ibis' range of food includes both terrestrial and aquatic invertebrates and human scraps. The most favoured foods are crayfish and mussels, which the bird obtains by digging with its long bill. Ibises have also been observed to eat cane toads by "flicking" them about to make them secrete their defensive toxin, then washing the toad in a nearby water source before consuming it. Researchers called this a learned behaviour "observed in multiple different regions". Although scientists do not think that the toxin affects most birds, they believe birds avoid it because of its "awful" taste.


Breeding

Breeding season varies with the location within Australia, generally August to November in the south, and February to May, after the wet season, in the north. The nest is a shallow dish-shaped platform of sticks, grasses or reeds, located in trees, generally near a body of water such as a river, swamp or lake. Ibises commonly nest near other waterbirds such as egrets, herons, spoonbills or cormorants. Two to three dull white eggs are laid measuring 65 mm × 44 mm (2.6 in × 1.7 in). The clutch is then incubated for 21–23 days. Hatchlings are altricial, that is, they are naked and helpless at birth, and take 48 days to fledge.
The mating patterns were extensively studied by Lowe, Beilharz and Evans on a wild population at Healesville Sanctuary, where they found that some birds were selecting the same nesting partner within and between years whilst others changed partners regularly. There were also many between-pair copulations.


In the Solomon Islands
The ibis is fairly common and is seen in groups of up to 30 birds along roads, on beaches and in the forest. It also visits villages where it forages with the domestic chickens. It feeds on the ground on invertebrates. When breeding, it nests in small colonies in forest trees and on islands in Lake Tegano.


In culture

The ibis has become a popular symbol of Australian identity, and has been depicted on television, in art, and in online memes. In March 2021, the Macquarie Dictionary blog chose "bin chicken" as an Australian word of the week, and wrote that it was potentially "competing with the kangaroo for the position of most iconic Australian animal".
In 1987 and 1989, respectively, the City of Griffith in the NSW Riverina region and the Municipality of Hunter's Hill on Sydney Harbour, both adopted a coat of arms granted by the College of Arms which feature two ibis birds as supporters.
In December 2017, the ibis placed second in Guardian Australia's inaugural Bird of the Year poll, a nationwide competition for Australia's favourite native bird. The ibis led the poll for much of the voting, but lost to the magpie by 843 votes (19,926 votes to 19,083).
In April 2022, Queensland sports minister Stirling Hinchliffe suggested the ibis as a potential mascot for the 2032 Olympic Games which are scheduled to be held in Brisbane.

In Brisbane, the Bin Chicken Trail is a sculpture trail by local artist Ryan Forster. It consists of a number of sculptures of bin chickens placed in various outdoor settings across Brisbane. Some of the sculputures have been stolen.


Relationship with humans

There has been debate in recent years over whether to consider them a pest or a possibly endangered species. Birds in tourist areas of Sydney, such as Darling Harbour, the Royal Botanic Gardens, and Centennial Park, have been a problem due to their strong smell. Populations in the last two areas have been culled.
The birds have also come to be regarded as a problem species in Victoria as a result of their scavenging activities, scattering rubbish from tips and bins in the process, and earning the widespread nickname "bin chicken". They are even known to snatch sandwiches from picnickers. Such behaviour, together with their propensity to build nests in "inappropriate" places, and competition with captive animals, led to surplus birds being relocated from Healesville Sanctuary to Sale. However, the birds returned in a few days.
They are a protected species in Queensland, New South Wales and Victoria.
While the birds can be a nuisance to some people in urban areas, their increased populations do not appear to carry ecological risks.


References


External links
 Media related to Threskiornis molucca at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Threskiornis moluccus at Wikispecies

The '''Australian white ibis''' ('''''Threskiornis molucca''''') is a wading [[bird]] of the [[family (biology)|family]] [[Threskiornithidae]]. It is widespread across much of [[Australia]]. It has a predominantly white plumage with a bare, black head, long downcurved bill, and black legs. While it is closely related to the [[African sacred ibis]], the Australian white ibis is a [[Birds of Australia|native Australian bird]]. Contrary to [[Urban legend|urban myth]], it is not a [[Introduced species|feral species]] introduced to Australia by people, and it does not come from Egypt.<ref name=":0" />

Historically rare in urban areas, the Australian white ibis has established in urban areas of the east coast in increasing numbers since the late 1970s; it is now commonly seen in [[Wollongong]], [[Sydney]], [[Melbourne]], [[Adelaide]], [[Darwin, Northern Territory|Darwin]], the [[Gold Coast, Queensland|Gold Coast]], [[Brisbane]] and [[Townsville]]. In recent years, the bird has also become increasingly common in [[Perth]], Western Australia, and surrounding towns in south-western Australia.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.dpaw.wa.gov.au/images/documents/plants-animals/animals/living-with-wildlife/ibis.pdf |title=Home | Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions |access-date=11 February 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160316084543/https://www.dpaw.wa.gov.au/images/documents/plants-animals/animals/living-with-wildlife/ibis.pdf |archive-date=16 March 2016 }}</ref> Populations have disappeared from natural breeding areas such as the [[Macquarie Marshes Nature Reserve|Macquarie Marshes]] in northern New South Wales. Management plans have been introduced to control problematic urban populations in Sydney.<ref>[http://www.bankstown.nsw.gov.au/index.aspx?NID=1301 Australian White Ibis] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160616170757/http://www.bankstown.nsw.gov.au/index.aspx?NID=1301 |date=16 June 2016 }} City of Canterbury Bankstown. Retrieved 22 December 2016.</ref><ref>[http://online.bankstown.nsw.gov.au/Docs/Common/Output/document.aspx?id=AvzKqrGh60I%3d Management Plan for Australian White Ibis in the Bankstown Local Government Area, 2012]{{Dead link|date=September 2023 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} Retrieved 22 December 2016.</ref>

Due to its increasing presence in the urban environment and its habit of rummaging in garbage, the species has acquired a variety of colloquial names such as "tip turkey"<ref name="abc1"/> and "bin chicken",<ref>[http://www.news.com.au/technology/science/animals/bin-chicken-set-to-be-australias-2017-bird-of-the-year/news-story/d1c08f8c6575d06844557c2112e0922f 'Bin chicken' set to be Australia's 2017 bird of the year] news.com.au 22 November 2017. Retrieved 7 September 2018.</ref> and in recent years has become an icon of Australia's popular culture, regarded with glee by some and passionate revulsion by others.<ref>Allatson, P and Connor, A. [http://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-09-07/ibis-bin-chicken-rise-totem-for-modern-australia/10209332  The rise of the ibis: How the 'bin chicken' became a totem for modern Australia] ABC News, 7 September 2018. Retrieved 7 September 2018.</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Stevens |first1=Rick |title=Bin chickens: the grotesque glory of the urban ibis – in pictures |url=https://www.theguardian.com/cities/gallery/2018/apr/09/bin-chickens-grotesque-glory-urban-ibis-in-pictures |access-date=3 January 2022 |work=The Guardian |date=9 April 2018}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
It was initially described by [[Georges Cuvier]] in 1829 as ''Ibis molucca''.<ref name="Cuvier-1829">{{cite book |last=Cuvier |first=Georges |author-link=Georges Cuvier |date=1829 |title=Le règne animal |trans-title=The Animal Kingdom |volume=I |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/18332#page/572 |location=Paris |publisher=chez Déterville & chez [[Masson_(publisher)|Crochard]] |page=520 |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.130093 |access-date=2025-05-28}}</ref> It is considered part of a [[Cryptic species complex|superspecies complex]] with the [[sacred ibis]] (''T. aethiopicus'') of Africa, and the [[black-headed ibis]] (''T. melanocephalus'') of Asia. Its status in the complex has vacillated over the years. Many older guidebooks referred to the bird as a species, ''T. molucca'', until a comprehensive review of plumage patterns by Holyoak in 1970. Holyoak noted the three species' similarities and that the Australian taxon resembled ''T. aethiopicus'' in adult plumage and ''T. melanocephalus'' in juvenile plumage. He proposed that they all be considered part of a single species, ''T. aethiopicus''. This was generally accepted by the scientific community until Lowe and Richards's assessment of plumage in 1991.<ref name="lowe91">{{cite journal |doi=10.1071/MU9910041 |vauthors=Lowe KW, Richards GC | year = 1991| title =Morphological Variation in the Sacred Ibis ''Threskiornis aethiopicus'' Superspecies Complex | journal =Emu | volume =91 | issue =1 | pages =41–45 |bibcode=1991EmuAO..91...41L | url =http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU9910041.htm | access-date = 14 June 2008| url-access =subscription }}</ref> They again recommended the recognition of ''molucca'' at species level. This was followed by a [[chromosome]] study, which highlighted each of the three species having a different [[karyotype]].<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=de Boer LE, van Brink JM | year =1982 | title =Cytotaxonomy of the Ciconiiformes (Aves), with karyotypes of eight species new to cytology | journal =Cytogenet. Cell Genet. | volume = 34| issue =1–2 | pages =19–34 | pmid =7151490 | doi=10.1159/000131791}}</ref> The Australian white ibis has been considered a full species by most authorities since then.<ref name=CB08>{{cite book |title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|url=https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri|url-access=limited|vauthors=Christidis L, Boles WE |year=2008 |publisher=CSIRO Publishing |location=Canberra |isbn=978-0-643-06511-6 |page=[https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri/page/n123 113]}}</ref>

===Subspecies===
Two subspecies are recognised:
* ''T. m. molucca'' of eastern [[Malesia]] and Australia, is the nominate subspecies.
* ''T. m. pygmaeus'' ('''Solomons white ibis'''), known locally as the ''tagoa'',<ref name=lt/> is a dwarf form found on the Solomon Islands that has been considered a separate species (as ''Threskiornis pygmaeus'') at times.<ref name=lowe91/> It is found only on [[Rennell Island|Rennell]] and [[Bellona Island]]s, in the [[Solomon Islands]] of [[Melanesia]], in the south-west [[Pacific Ocean]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/australian-white-ibis-threskiornis-molucca |title=Australian White Ibis (''Threskiornis molucca'') |access-date=2011-12-21 |work=HBW 1, p. 493 |publisher=Lynx Editions – Internet Bird Collection }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author1=Lowe, Kim W. |author2=Richards, Geraldine C. |name-list-style=amp |year=1991 |title=Morphological Variation in the Sacred Ibis ''Threskiornis aethiopicus'' Superspecies Cornplex |journal=Emu |volume=91 |issue=1 |pages=41–45 |doi=10.1071/MU9910041|bibcode=1991EmuAO..91...41L }}</ref>

==Description==

The Australian white ibis is a fairly large [[ibis]] species, around {{convert|65|–|75|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and has a bald black head and neck and a long black downcurved beak, measuring over {{convert|16.7|cm|in|abbr=on}} in the male, and under in the female. There is some [[sexual dimorphism]] in size, as the slightly heavier male weighs {{convert|1.7|–|2.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} compared to the {{convert|1.4|–|1.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}} female.<ref name="booklet">{{cite web|url=http://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/resources/nature/wildAboutIbis.pdf |title=Wild about ibis: living with urban wildlife |access-date=15 June 2008 |veditors=Legoe C, Ross G |date=April 2007 |work=Department of Environment and Climate Change, New South Wales – website |publisher=Department of Environment and Climate Change NSW| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20080727034158/http://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/resources/nature/wildAboutIbis.pdf| archive-date= 27 July 2008 | url-status= live}}</ref> As a comparison, the [[American white ibis]] generally attains {{convert|1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in weight.<ref name= Hancock>Hancock & Kushan, ''Storks, Ibises and Spoonbills of the World''. Princeton University Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0-12-322730-0}}</ref> The body [[plumage]] is white, although it may become brown-stained. Inner secondary plumes are displayed as lacy black "tail" feathers. The upper tail becomes yellow when the bird is breeding. The legs and feet are dark and red skin is visible on the underside of the wing. Immature birds have shorter bills.<ref name = "Simpsday">{{cite book| vauthors = Simpson K, Day N, Trusler P | title = Field Guide to the Birds of Australia | publisher = Viking O'Neil | year = 1993 | location = Ringwood, Victoria | page = 54 | isbn = 978-0-670-90478-5}}</ref> The head and neck are feathered in juveniles.

</gallery>
The call is a long [[Frog|croak]].<ref name = "Simpsday"/>

The Australian white ibis reaches sexual maturity in three years,<ref name=booklet/> and can reach twenty-eight years of age.<ref name="abc1"/>

==Distribution==
[[File:Threskiornis molucca -Coolart Wetlands, Mornington Peninsula, Australia-8.jpg|thumb|Adult at [[Coolart Wetlands]], [[Mornington Peninsula]], Australia]]
[[File:Australian Ibis, Building 17, Douglas QLD 4814, Australia imported from iNaturalist photo 447727837.jpg|thumb|Flying in a [[V formation]]]]
The Australian white ibis is widespread in eastern, northern and south-western [[Australia]]. It occurs in marshy wetlands, often near open grasslands and has become common in Australian east-coast city parks and rubbish dumps in the urban areas of [[Wollongong]], [[Sydney]], [[Perth, Western Australia|Perth]], the [[Gold Coast, Queensland|Gold Coast]], [[Brisbane]] and [[Townsville]]. Historically, it was rare in urban areas – the first visits were noted after drought drove birds eastwards in the late 1970s, and there were no breeding records in Sydney until the 1980s.<ref name="abc.net.au">{{Cite web|date=2018-08-07|title=Is Taronga Zoo responsible for Sydney's prolific bin chicken population?|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-08-08/is-taronga-zoo-responsible-sydney-ibis-bin-chicken-population/10078786|access-date=2020-10-06|publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation|language=en-AU}}</ref>

The [[Macquarie Marshes Nature Reserve|Macquarie Marshes]] in north-western New South Wales used to be one of the main breeding areas of the bird, with 11,000 nests reported in 1998. However, since 2000, none have been reported breeding there.<ref name="abc1"/> The species is absent from Tasmania.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://birdlife.org.au/bird-profiles/|title=Bird profiles|website=BirdLife Australia}}</ref>

Populations are known to have boomed, particularly in urban areas, in the 1970s, early 2000s, and around 2010.<ref name=":1">{{Cite news |last=Caldwell |first=Ima |date=2025-05-24 |title=Bin chickens galore! Why are there so many ibis in Sydney? |url=https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2025/may/24/why-are-there-so-many-ibis-in-sydney-bin-chicken-population |access-date=2025-05-27 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> However, there is no national monitoring of the species, and therefore no official population counts.<ref name=":1" />

===Urban populations===
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image='Bin chicken' (Threskiornis molucca) in Belmore Park, Sydney.jpg|caption=On top of a bin in Belmore Park, Sydney, 2025|desired_width=250|align=right|crop_top_perc=40|crop_bottom_perc=10|crop_right_perc=30|crop_left_perc=10}}
The species has been able to colonise urban areas by reducing its fear response when in close proximity to humans, and by significantly widening its suite of food items to include human refuse – strategies that other closely related species such as the [[straw-necked ibis]] and the [[spoonbill]]s have not replicated.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Denby |first=Matthew |date=2020-10-31 |title=Secrets of the Ibis: The surprising real reason 'bin chickens' took Sydney by storm |url=https://sydneysentinel.com.au/2020/10/secrets-of-the-ibis-the-surprising-real-reason-bin-chickens-took-sydney-by-storm/ |access-date=2020-10-31 |website=The Sydney Sentinel |language=en-AU |archive-date=2 March 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230302001432/https://sydneysentinel.com.au/2020/10/secrets-of-the-ibis-the-surprising-real-reason-bin-chickens-took-sydney-by-storm/ |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref>

Questions surrounding the origins of recent highly urbanised and closely human-habituated populations of the species are complicated by the establishment of free-flying exhibit flocks of formerly captive birds at a number of zoos and wildlife parks, including Sydney's [[Taronga Zoo Sydney|Taronga Zoo]], which first acquired birds for this purpose around 1971.<ref name="abc.net.au"/><ref name=":0"/> A 1973 [[ABC Television (Australian TV network)|ABC TV]] report noted Taronga's by then well-established "liberty flock" was breeding locally, unlike natural populations, which at that time were only known to fleetingly visit the urban area and not breed there.<ref>{{Citation|title=Weekend Magazine: Ibis (1973)|url=https://www.facebook.com/abcinsydney/videos/weekend-magazine-ibis-1973/10159279767799988/|language=en|access-date=2020-10-06}}</ref>

The resident Taronga flock nested in exotic [[Phoenix canariensis|Canary Island date palms]], and was notably very closely habituated to people; approaching them at close quarters, feeding from rubbish bins and scavenging food from outdoor dining areas. These previously undocumented behaviours became closely associated with the urban Sydney flocks that emerged from around 1980 onwards, directly across the harbour in the [[Royal Botanic Garden, Sydney|Royal Botanic Gardens]] and the [[Sydney central business district|Sydney CBD]], and further afield in the [[Centennial Parklands]].<ref name="booklet" /><ref name=":0"/>

The emergence of urban ibis populations in the wake of the 1970s zoo liberty flock experiment directly mirrors the establishment of the African sacred ibis as a resident species across Europe, with those populations confirmed as originating from free ranging zoo flocks in the same decade.<ref>{{Cite journal|title="Sacred Ibis: a new invasive species in Europe"|last=Yésou, Pierre; Clergeau, Philippe|date=2005|journal=Birding World|issue=12|volume=18|pages=517–526.|url=https://www.birdingworld.co.uk/images/SacredIbises.pdf}}</ref>

The relationship between birds originating from the Taronga flock and any influx of inland birds into Sydney is poorly known, but it is speculated that human-habituated flocks originating from the zoo may have encouraged some visiting flocks to stay in Sydney, with the two populations likely merging to some degree.<ref name="abc.net.au"/> Resident Sydney birds may have influenced newcomers fleeing from inland drought to adapt to new food sources in the city and to accept close proximity to humans.<ref name=":0"/>

Other free-flying exhibit populations were similarly established at [[Healesville Sanctuary]] in Victoria and at [[Tidbinbilla Nature Reserve|Tidbinbilla]] in the ACT, both of which mirrored the Taronga example by appearing to serve as population nucleation points.<ref name="abc.net.au" /> Healesville birds also seeded a free-flying population at [[Currumbin Wildlife Sanctuary]] in Queensland.<ref name=":0" />

The urban population further increased after a further period of inland drought in 1998.<ref name="abc1">{{cite web
| publisher = [[Australian Broadcasting Corporation]]
| url = http://www.abc.net.au/science/scribblygum/november2007/
| title = Ibis invasion
| date = 15 November 2007
| access-date = 22 November 2007
| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20071116190756/http://www.abc.net.au/science/scribblygum/november2007/| archive-date= 16 November 2007 | url-status= live}}</ref> The birds are an example of wildlife migration caused by climate change.<ref name=":1"/> The first big colony set up in the Sydney suburb of [[Bankstown]] and started to cause anxiety in the local community. It is estimated the colony was the largest outside the Macquarie Marshes, their natural breeding wetland in inland NSW.<ref name="abc1"/>

==Behaviour==
===Feeding===
[[File:Threskiornis molucca -Coolart Wetlands, Mornington Peninsula, Australia -nest and eggs-8.jpg|right|thumb|Nesting at [[Coolart Wetlands and Homestead Reserve|Coolart Wetlands]], [[Mornington Peninsula]], Australia]]The Australian white ibis' range of food includes both terrestrial and aquatic invertebrates and human scraps. The most favoured foods are crayfish and mussels, which the bird obtains by digging with its long bill. Ibises have also been observed to eat [[cane toad]]s by "flicking" them about to make them secrete their defensive toxin, then washing the toad in a nearby water source before consuming it. Researchers called this a learned behaviour "observed in multiple different regions". Although scientists do not think that the toxin affects most birds, they believe birds avoid it because of its "awful" taste.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2022-11-23/ibis-find-way-to-eat-toxic-cane-toads/101683596|title=Ibis use 'stress and wash' technique to eat poisonous cane toads|website=ABC|last=Forbes|first=Tom|date=23 November 2022|access-date=25 November 2022}}</ref>

===Breeding===
{{Easy CSS image crop|Image=Australian Ibis, Toowoomba QLD, Australia imported from iNaturalist photo 324612217.jpg|desired_width=250|align=right|crop_right_perc=40|crop_left_perc=41|crop_top_perc=35|crop_bottom_perc=30|caption=Mating, in [[Toowoomba]], Queensland}}
Breeding season varies with the location within Australia, generally August to November in the south, and February to May, after the [[wet season]], in the north. The nest is a shallow dish-shaped platform of sticks, grasses or reeds, located in trees, generally near a body of water such as a river, swamp or lake. Ibises commonly nest near other waterbirds such as egrets, herons, spoonbills or cormorants. Two to three dull white eggs are laid measuring {{Convert|65 x 44|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite book |author= Beruldsen, Gordon | title = Australian Birds: Their Nests and Eggs | publisher = self | year = 2003 | location = Kenmore Hills, Qld | page = 177 | isbn = 978-0-646-42798-0 | author-link = Gordon Beruldsen}}</ref> The clutch is then incubated for 21–23 days. Hatchlings are [[altricial]], that is, they are naked and helpless at birth, and take 48 days to [[fledge]].<ref name="booklet" />

The mating patterns were extensively studied by Lowe, Beilharz and Evans on a wild population at Healesville Sanctuary, where they found that some birds were selecting the same nesting partner within and between years whilst others changed partners regularly. There were also many between-pair copulations.<ref>{{cite thesis|title=The feeding and breeding biology of the sacred ibis Threskiornis aethiopicus in southern Victoria|first=Kim Waldock|last=Lowe|url=https://minerva-access.unimelb.edu.au/items/978d1c84-1655-5956-b47a-9025a7a3d0e7/full|type=PhD|year=1984|publisher=University of Melbourne}}</ref>

===In the Solomon Islands===
The ibis is fairly common and is seen in groups of up to 30 birds along roads, on beaches and in the forest. It also visits villages where it forages with the domestic [[chicken]]s. It feeds on the ground on [[invertebrate]]s. When breeding, it nests in small colonies in forest trees and on islands in [[Lake Tegano]].<ref name=lt>{{cite web|url=http://tegano-lake.com/Birdlife.html|title=Bird Life|access-date=2011-12-23|work=Welcome to Lake Tegano|publisher=Tegano-lake.com|year=2009|archive-date=4 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304071604/http://tegano-lake.com/Birdlife.html}}</ref>

==In culture==
[[File:Ibis art Sydney.png|thumb|Graffiti art depicting an Australian white ibis in inner western Sydney]]
The ibis has become a popular symbol of Australian identity, and has been depicted on television, in art, and in online memes.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Goulis |first1=Leah |title='What's a bin chicken?' US Bluey fans are confused by Australian slang |url=https://www.kidspot.com.au/lifestyle/entertainment/whats-a-bin-chicken-us-bluey-fans-are-confused-by-australian-slang/news-story/bac151e6cd1e3c0bad622028bb23833d |access-date=17 January 2023 |work=Kidspot |publisher=News.com.au}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Allatson |first1=Paul |last2=Connor |first2=Andrea |title=How the 'bin chicken' became a totem for modern Australia |url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2018-09-07/ibis-bin-chicken-rise-totem-for-modern-australia/10209332 |publisher=ABC News |access-date=7 September 2018 |language=en-AU |date=6 September 2018}}</ref> In March 2021, the ''[[Macquarie Dictionary]]'' blog chose "bin chicken" as an Australian word of the week, and wrote that it was potentially "competing with the kangaroo for the position of most iconic Australian animal".<ref>{{cite web |title=All hail the bin chicken |url=https://www.macquariedictionary.com.au/blog/article/781/ |website=Macquarie Dictionary Blog |date=2 November 2021 |publisher=Macquarie Dictionary |access-date=17 January 2023}}</ref>

In 1987 and 1989, respectively, the [[City of Griffith]] in the NSW [[Riverina]] region and the [[Municipality of Hunter's Hill]] on [[Sydney Harbour]], both adopted a coat of arms granted by the [[College of Arms]] which feature two ibis birds as [[Supporters (heraldry)|supporters]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Hoopman |first1=Sue |title=Our Coat of Arms defines who we are |url=https://www.weeklytimes.com.au/our-coat-of-arms-defines-who-we-are/ |access-date=2 September 2023 |publisher=The Weekly Times |date=13 September 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Williams |first1=Ross |title=Mayors Of Hunter's Hill 1861 to 2012 |url=https://www.huntershill.nsw.gov.au/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/MayorsofHuntersHillV12.pdf |publisher=Hunter's Hill Council |access-date=2 September 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230618235914/https://www.huntershill.nsw.gov.au/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/MayorsofHuntersHillV12.pdf |archive-date=18 June 2023 |date=29 February 2012}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Schofield |first1=Chris |title=Coat of arms reflects the unique culture of historic municipality |url=http://huntershill.nsw.au/neighbourhood/heritage/hunters-hill-coat-of-arms/ |website=Discover Hunters Hill |publisher=The Weekly Times |access-date=2 September 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160312182705/http://huntershill.nsw.au/neighbourhood/heritage/hunters-hill-coat-of-arms/ |archive-date=12 March 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Baskerville |first1=Bruce |last2=Szabo |first2=Stephen |title=Regional Visit 2007 - City of Griffith and the Murrumbidgee Irrigation Area (MIA): Some Notes on the Civic, Ecclesiastical and Vineyard Heraldry of the Griffith District |url=http://www.heritage.nsw.gov.au/docs/heraldry_griffith_mia.pdf |publisher=Heritage Council of NSW |access-date=13 June 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110329025048/http://www.heritage.nsw.gov.au/docs/heraldry_griffith_mia.pdf |archive-date=29 March 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Our history - Coat of arms |url=https://www.griffith.nsw.gov.au/Council/About-Council/Our-history |publisher=Griffith City Council |access-date=13 June 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250414134703/https://www.griffith.nsw.gov.au/Council/About-Council/Our-history |archive-date=14 April 2025}}</ref>

In December 2017, the ibis placed second in ''[[Guardian Australia]]''{{'s}} inaugural [[Australian Bird of the Year|Bird of the Year]] poll, a nationwide competition for Australia's favourite native bird. The ibis led the poll for much of the voting, but lost to the [[Australian magpie|magpie]] by 843 votes (19,926 votes to 19,083).<ref>{{cite news |last1=Langford |first1=Sam |title=Bin Chickens Are Leading in Australia's Bird of the Year Vote, And It's Time To Have Your Say |url=https://junkee.com/bird-of-the-year-bin-chicken-guardian/136047 |access-date=17 January 2023 |work=Junkee |date=21 November 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Wahlquist |first1=Calla |title=Magpie edges out white ibis and kookaburra as Australian bird of the year |url=https://www.theguardian.com/environment/2017/dec/11/magpie-edges-out-white-ibis-and-kookaburra-as-australian-bird-of-the-year |access-date=17 January 2023 |work=The Guardian |date=10 December 2017 |language=en}}</ref>

In April 2022, Queensland sports minister [[Stirling Hinchliffe]] suggested the ibis as a potential mascot for the [[2032 Olympic Games]] which are scheduled to be held in [[Brisbane]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=McKay |first1=Jack |title="Stirling Hinchliffe suggests ibis should be contender for 2032 Games mascot" |url=https://www.couriermail.com.au/sport/seq-olympics-2032/stirling-hinchliffe-suggests-ibis-should-be-contender-for-2032-games-mascot/news-story/baf7b6c763680449da753c82c666a765 |access-date=24 July 2022 |work=The Courier-Mail |date=4 April 2022}}</ref>
[[File:Sculpture of Walter, the Bin Chicken (Sacred Isis), Indooroopilly, 2026.jpg|thumb|Sculpture of Walter the Bin Chicken at Indooroopilly, named after the Walter Taylor Bridge which is nearby, 2026]]
In Brisbane, the Bin Chicken Trail is a [[sculpture trail]] by local artist Ryan Forster. It consists of a number of sculptures of bin chickens placed in various outdoor settings across Brisbane. Some of the sculputures have been stolen.<ref>{{Cite web |title=This Bin Chicken Trail By A Local Artist Is Brisbane’s Newest Tourist Attraction |url=https://www.theurbanlist.com/brisbane/a-list/bin-chicken-trail-brisbane |access-date=2026-02-27 |website=The Urban List |language=en|date=13 March 2024|last=Laine|first=Ranyhyn}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Bin Chicken Trail |url=https://www.sethius.art/binny |access-date=2026-02-27 |website=Sethius Art |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=O'Malley |first=Brendan |date=20 July 2023 |title=Bin chicken sculptures kidnapped, but artist vows more are coming to Brisbane |url=https://www.couriermail.com.au/questnews/southwest/bin-chicken-sculptures-kidnapped-but-artist-vows-more-are-coming-to-brisbane/news-story/5224c871e42a65f8d0370351fddf4c0d |access-date=27 February 2026 |work=[[The Courier-Mail]]}}</ref>

==Relationship with humans==
[[File:TipTurkeyOrBinChickenSydney2012.jpg|thumb|Perching on a wheelie bin in Potts Point, Sydney, 2012]]
There has been debate in recent years over whether to consider them a pest or a possibly endangered species. Birds in tourist areas of Sydney, such as [[Darling Harbour]], the [[Royal Botanic Gardens, Sydney|Royal Botanic Gardens]], and [[Centennial Park, New South Wales|Centennial Park]], have been a problem due to their strong smell. Populations in the last two areas have been culled.<ref name="smh1">{{cite news
| work = [[The Sydney Morning Herald]]
| url = http://blogs.smh.com.au/science/archives/2006/10/_they_seem_to_b.html
| title = Ibises–cull them or cuddle them?
| access-date = 5 November 2006
| date = 5 October 2006
| archive-date = 4 March 2016
| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160304001813/http://blogs.smh.com.au/science/archives/2006/10/_they_seem_to_b.html
}}</ref>

The birds have also come to be regarded as a problem species in [[Victoria, Australia|Victoria]] as a result of their scavenging activities, scattering rubbish from tips and bins in the process, and earning the widespread nickname "bin chicken". They are even known to snatch sandwiches from picnickers. Such behaviour, together with their propensity to build nests in "inappropriate" places, and competition with captive animals, led to surplus birds being relocated from [[Healesville Sanctuary]] to [[Sale, Victoria|Sale]]. However, the birds returned in a few days.<ref name="Temby">{{cite web|last=Temby |first=Ian |publisher=State of Victoria Department of Sustainability and Environment |date=June 2003 |title=Problems caused by the Australian White Ibis |url=http://www.dpi.vic.gov.au/DPI/nreninf.nsf/9e58661e880ba9e44a256c640023eb2e/eca25163a519edfaca256e7200224149/$FILE/FF0005.pdf |access-date=29 June 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080816030817/http://www.dpi.vic.gov.au/DPI/nreninf.nsf/9e58661e880ba9e44a256c640023eb2e/eca25163a519edfaca256e7200224149/%24FILE/FF0005.pdf |archive-date=16 August 2008 }}</ref>

They are a protected species in Queensland,<ref>{{cite web |title=Australian White Ibis |url=https://www.brisbane.qld.gov.au/clean-and-green/natural-environment-and-water/biodiversity-in-brisbane/wildlife-in-brisbane/living-with-wildlife/australian-white-ibis |website=www.brisbane.qld.gov.au |date=10 May 2019 |language=en}}</ref> New South Wales<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.cbcity.nsw.gov.au/environment/biodiversity/native-animals/australian-white-ibis|title=Australian White Ibis|website=www.cbcity.nsw.gov.au}}</ref> and Victoria.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.wildlife.vic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0021/542217/Australian-White-Ibis.pdf|title=Australian White Ibis Factsheet|publisher=Victoria Department of Energy, Environment and Climate Action}}</ref>

While the birds can be a nuisance to some people in urban areas, their increased populations do not appear to carry ecological risks.<ref name=":1" />

zh.extract:
澳洲白䴉（學名：Threskiornis moluccus）為䴉科白䴉屬的鳥類，外表和體型皆與同屬的埃及聖䴉非常相近，主要分布於摩鹿加群島東部、新幾內亞、索羅門群島拉納爾和貝羅那省、澳洲，近代在塔斯馬尼亞與紐西蘭部分地區亦有愈來愈多的出沒紀錄。
和其他白䴉一樣，澳洲白䴉偏好棲息於水邊，包含潟湖、濕地、沼澤、河岸灘地等等。而澳洲的族群亦常見於人類活動密集的地區，從牧場、公園草坪、鋪設草皮的球場到公共垃圾箱都能遇見，也因為在大城市中常見牠們用細長的喙在垃圾筒中覓食，而在當地有「垃圾雞」（bin chicken, trash turkey）的俗稱。


行為特徵
成年的澳洲白䴉頭部及雙腳為烏黑色，翅膀末端有一條黑色的斑紋。
